front: Philosopher: outlined four 'idols of the mind' — Tribe, Cave, Marketplace, and Theatre — as sources of false reasoning in a work on the scientific method
work: Novum Organum and the Scientific Method

front: Work: championed 'true induction' over Aristotelian syllogisms; title references Aristotle's collected works on logic (the Organon)
work: Novum Organum and the Scientific Method

front: Philosopher: arranged data into 'tables of presentation' — presence, absence, and degrees — to investigate natural phenomena like heat, which this thinker described as 'motion and nothing else'
work: Novum Organum and the Scientific Method

front: Work: planned sixfold reformation of all knowledge, of which only the Novum Organum was completed; aimed to reform knowledge in six parts like God's six days of creation
back: The Great Instauration / Instauratio Magna (Francis Bacon)

front: Work: classifies knowledge into history, poesy, and philosophy; identifies 'delicate,' 'contentious,' and 'fantastical' as three 'distempers' of false learning; inspired the classification in Diderot's Encyclopedie
Work: utopian novel set on the island of Bensalem, featuring an ideal research institute called Salomon's House with 'Merchants of Light' and 'Inoculaters'
work: The New Atlantis and Salomon's House

front: Philosopher: analogized 'empirics' to ants and 'dogmatical' thinkers to spiders, but likened the 'true labor of philosophy' to a bee gathering honey
Philosopher: listed 27 kinds of 'prerogative instances' including 'Instances of the Fingerpost'; cited by John R. Platt as inspiration for his essay 'Strong Inference'
work: Novum Organum and the Scientific Method

front: Philosopher: Voltaire's twelfth Philosophical Letter praises this thinker as the 'father of experimental philosophy'
Concept: idols named for received philosophical systems likened to 'stage plays that misrepresent the world,' one of four categories including Tribe, Cave, and Marketplace
work: Novum Organum and the Scientific Method
